papers.title: ‘Unaffordable’ visa price hike threatens Australia’s researcher pipeline
papers.body_text: An increase in the price of an Australian visa might make it more difficult for graduates to stay in the nation after they complete their degree.Credit: Bianca De Marchi /AAP/Alamy University and postgraduate groups in Australia are urging their government to exempt PhD graduates from the surprise doubling of a key visa fee, warning that the price increase will drive international research talent away. On 1 March, the Australian Department of Home Affairs said that the fee charged for higher-education temporary graduate visas (a category called subclass 485) would double with immediate effect, from Aus$2,300 (around US$1,600) to Aus$4,600. The visa allows international students who have graduated from Australian universities less than six months previously to remain in the nation for a few years (usually two or three depending on the type of qualification they receive) after completing their degrees. It lets graduates either continue their studies or find work and can serve as a bridge for those transitioning from PhD programmes to postdoctoral positions or into other research careers. On 10 March, the Council of Australian Postgraduate Associations (CAPA) wrote to Tony Burke, the home affairs minister, arguing that the increased fee risks cutting off Australia’s “critical pipeline” of foreign research talent. The organization called on the government to exempt people who are completing research-based master’s or PhD degrees from the price hike. For PhD students who would like to stay in Australia after graduating, this visa is, on average, the equivalent of 13% of their yearly stipend. PhD students already cite cost of living as their biggest concern, CAPA said. “Doubling the cost of their ticket into early-career research … will turn the talent elsewhere,” said the organization. Australia must boost R&D investment to reclaim global research standing In 2023, international candidates made up 40% of all PhD enrolments in Australia, compared with an average of 25% in 2022 across Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) nations. The visa fee hike could add to other pressures that universities face when recruiting research talent, such as a lack of funding available for PhD stipends — which often sit close to the poverty line — and a cap on university spending that limits the proportion of doctoral funding that can be offered to international students to 10%. One international PhD student at an Australian university, who asked not to be identified owing to concerns about future visa applications, told Nature that the increased fees might affect the pipeline of postdocs into Australian research. “Universities will potentially lose some often-exceptional candidates that can’t afford to put that much money upfront, way before they start getting paid,” he said. Extra measures The visa fee increase is the latest in a series of tightened immigration measures flowing from a migration strategy launched by the Australian government in December 2023. The strategy aimed to fix what it described as a “broken” migration system, tackling serial ‘visa hopping’, a tactic that enabled visitors to stay in the nation for a long time by moving between visa types. The measures also seek to protect Australia’s higher-education system from “unscrupulous” actors, including “ghost schools” that allow students to enroll without attending real classes as a route to live and work in the nation. Since then, changes to migration policies have come in quick succession. In 2024, the maximum eligible age for the 485 visa was reduced from 50 to 35, although postgraduates with research-based degrees were exempt from the change. From January this year, further restrictions on visa hopping will prevent holders of 485 and visitor visas from applying for student visas while in Australia.

questions.stem: The primary reason Australian universities and postgraduate groups are urging the government to exempt PhD graduates from the visa fee increase is to ____.

options.A: maintain the current cost of living for international students.
options.B: prevent a decline in international research talent entering the country.
options.C: encourage more graduates to pursue undergraduate degrees.
options.D: simplify the visa application process for all graduates.

questions.explanation: The article states that universities and postgraduate groups are warning that the price increase will 'drive international research talent away,' indicating their primary concern is retaining skilled researchers.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the temporary graduate visa (subclass 485) as described in the text?

options.A: To allow international students to start their studies in Australia immediately after graduation.
options.B: To provide a pathway for international students to work in Australia after completing their degrees and potentially pursue further research.
options.C: To enable recent graduates to find temporary housing while searching for permanent employment.
options.D: To facilitate the transition of Australian citizens into postgraduate programs overseas.

questions.explanation: The text explains that the visa 'allows international students who have graduated from Australian universities... to remain in the nation for a few years... after completing their degrees. It lets graduates either continue their studies or find work and can serve as a bridge for those transitioning from PhD programmes to postdoctoral positions or into other research careers.'

questions.stem: According to the Council of Australian Postgraduate Associations (CAPA), the increased visa fee for PhD graduates represents a significant financial burden because ____.

options.A: it exceeds the average annual stipend of a PhD student.
options.B: it is disproportionately high compared to other visa application costs.
options.C: it constitutes a substantial percentage of their yearly earnings.
options.D: it must be paid upfront before any research funding is received.

questions.explanation: CAPA states that 'For PhD students who would like to stay in Australia after graduating, this visa is, on average, the equivalent of 13% of their yearly stipend.' This indicates it's a considerable portion of their income.

questions.stem: The article mentions that in 2023, international candidates constituted a larger proportion of PhD enrolments in Australia compared to the average across OECD nations. This statistic is used to highlight ____.

options.A: Australia's declining attractiveness for international students.
options.B: the success of previous government policies in attracting global talent.
options.C: the potential negative impact of the visa fee hike on Australia's research landscape.
options.D: the increasing cost of doctoral education in OECD countries.

questions.explanation: By presenting Australia's high percentage of international PhD students in 2023, the article sets a context that makes the subsequent concern about driving talent away due to increased fees more impactful, suggesting a potential decline from this strong position.

questions.stem: The Australian government's recent migration strategy, launched in December 2023, aims to address issues such as 'broken' migration systems and 'visa hopping'. The increase in the temporary graduate visa fee can be seen as ____.

options.A: an unrelated policy change occurring simultaneously with the strategy.
options.B: a measure designed to curb specific immigration tactics and reinforce the strategy's goals.
options.C: an initiative to encourage more international students to stay permanently in Australia.
options.D: a response to pressure from international research talent to reduce visa costs.

questions.explanation: The article explicitly states, 'The visa fee increase is the latest in a series of tightened immigration measures flowing from a migration strategy launched by the Australian government in December 2023. The strategy aimed to fix what it described as a 'broken' migration system, tackling serial ‘visa hopping’...' This directly links the fee increase as part of the broader strategy to control migration.

questions.stem: Based on the provided text, which of the following regions is NOT explicitly mentioned as being significantly impacted or discussed in relation to the 'Iran war' or its consequences?

options.A: The United States
options.B: Europe
options.C: South America
options.D: Asia

questions.explanation: The text details the 'Iran war's' impact on the United States (hurting Donald Trump, military power, etc.), Europe (energy problems), and Asia (dragging allies in, Indian gas scramble, etc.). South America is not directly mentioned in connection to the Iran war's effects within this context.

questions.stem: The article 'War in Iran is making Donald Trump weaker—and angrier' suggests a potential correlation between the Iran war and Donald Trump's political standing. What is the primary sentiment conveyed about Trump's reaction to this situation?

options.A: He is calmly strategizing diplomatic solutions.
options.B: He is becoming more politically vulnerable and irritable.
options.C: He is focusing on economic recovery measures.
options.D: He is delegating war responsibilities to allies.

questions.explanation: The headline explicitly states the war is making Trump 'weaker—and angrier,' directly indicating a negative impact on his political strength and an increase in his negative emotional state.

questions.stem: According to the text, which of the following is presented as a potential consequence of the Iran war for American military power?

options.A: It will lead to a significant reduction in military spending.
options.B: It could deplete American military capabilities for an extended period.
options.C: It will necessitate a complete withdrawal from global military engagements.
options.D: It will result in a swift and decisive victory, enhancing US influence.

questions.explanation: The article mentions 'The Iran war could sap American military power for years,' directly suggesting a long-term depletion of capabilities.

questions.stem: The article mentions that 'Public opinion in China is hardening on America and Taiwan.' What does this suggest about the relationship between China, America, and Taiwan in the context of the broader geopolitical landscape presented?

options.A: China is seeking closer ties with both America and Taiwan.
options.B: China's stance towards America and Taiwan is becoming more rigid and potentially confrontational.
options.C: China is advocating for de-escalation and peaceful resolution of disputes.
options.D: China views America and Taiwan as strategic partners in regional security.

questions.explanation: The phrase 'hardening on' implies a strengthening of negative or resistant attitudes, indicating a more inflexible and potentially adversarial position from China regarding America and Taiwan.

questions.stem: Which of the following business sectors is explicitly mentioned as potentially undergoing significant transformation due to the Iran war?

options.A: Technology and Artificial Intelligence
options.B: Renewable Energy and Gas
options.C: Airline Industry
options.D: Luxury Fashion Retail

questions.explanation: Under the 'Business' section, the article states, 'War may bring lasting change to the airline business,' directly linking the war to potential shifts in this industry.

papers.title: Madagascar’s military ruler decrees that ministers must pass lie detector tests
papers.body_text: Madagascar’s military president has said new ministers will have to pass lie detector tests to root out corrupt candidates, after he dismissed the prime minister and cabinet without explanation earlier this month. Michael Randrianirina came to power in a coup in October after weeks of youth-led protests under the banner “Gen Z Madagascar”. However, young people were quickly disenchanted by his choice of government officials, which they saw as being part of the old, corrupt elite. Randrianirina told local media: “We have decided to use a polygraph. It is with this polygraph that the background integrity checks will be carried out.” The president said a new cabinet would be announced early next week. “We will know who is corrupt and who can help us, who is going to betray the youth struggle,” he said. View image in fullscreen A youth protester throws a teargas canister back at security forces during clashes in October. Photograph: Luis Tato/AFP/Getty Images Malagasy young people started protesting in September last year, first against water and power cuts, then demanding a complete overhaul of the political system. At least 22 people were killed in the first days of the protests, according to the UN. On 11 October, the elite military unit Capsat, in which Randrianirina was a colonel, came out in support of protesters. The next day, the president, Andry Rajoelina, reportedly fled the country for Dubai on a French military plane. Randrianirina was sworn in as interim president and has pledged to hold elections by late 2027. Gen Z activists have been pushing him to confirm the date, while criticising his appointments over their perceived ties to the previous regime. Randrianirina fired the prime minister and cabinet on 9 March, then announced on Sunday that the anti-corruption chief, Mamitiana Rajaonarison, would be the new prime minister. He and Rajaonarison would only interview ministerial candidates who passed a lie detector test, he said on Thursday. He said: “We’re not looking for someone who is 100% clean, but over 60%. That way, Madagascar will finally be able to develop.” One of the managers of Gen Z Madagascar’s social media accounts expressed scepticism at the use of polygraphs. “It’s not even scientifically proven to work,” he said. “For me it’s just a joke and embarrassing.” He added: “We agree that the previous ministers weren’t good. We still have hope for the new ministers, but in general I think this regime is already better than the regime of Andry Rajoelina.” Madagascar is one of the world’s poorest countries, with a GDP per capita of just $545 (£408) in 2024, according to World Bank data. The island is rich in natural resources, including vanilla and precious gems, which campaigners say have been exploited by officials and corrupt businesspeople. The country ranked 148 out of 180 countries in Transparency International’s 2025 corruption perceptions index. Agence-France Presse and Associated Press contributed to this report

questions.stem: What is the primary reason cited for the dismissal of the prime minister and cabinet by Madagascar's military president?

options.A: Lack of explanation for their policies.
options.B: Allegations of corruption and dissatisfaction with government appointments.
options.C: Failure to address youth-led protests effectively.
options.D: Disagreements over the implementation of lie detector tests.

questions.explanation: The article states that the president dismissed the prime minister and cabinet, and later announced new ministers would need to pass lie detector tests to 'root out corrupt candidates'. It also mentions that young people were 'disenchanted by his choice of government officials, which they saw as being part of the old, corrupt elite'. While youth protests were a catalyst for the change in power, the immediate reason for the cabinet dismissal and the subsequent policy is related to corruption concerns.

questions.stem: What is the proposed method to ensure the integrity of new ministerial candidates in Madagascar?

options.A: Public scrutiny of their past actions.
options.B: Background checks conducted by independent auditors.
options.C: Passing lie detector (polygraph) tests.
options.D: Interviews with representatives of 'Gen Z Madagascar'.

questions.explanation: The president, Michael Randrianirina, explicitly stated, 'We have decided to use a polygraph. It is with this polygraph that the background integrity checks will be carried out.' He further elaborated that he and the new prime minister would 'only interview ministerial candidates who passed a lie detector test'.

questions.stem: What was the initial cause of the youth-led protests in Madagascar, according to the article?

options.A: The president's decision to dismiss the cabinet.
options.B: The perceived ties of new appointments to the previous regime.
options.C: Discontent with the country's economic performance.
options.D: Protests against water and power cuts.

questions.explanation: The article mentions that 'Malagasy young people started protesting in September last year, first against water and power cuts, then demanding a complete overhaul of the political system.' While later protests evolved to include political system demands and dissatisfaction with appointments, the initial trigger was related to basic utilities.

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the sentiment of some 'Gen Z Madagascar' activists regarding the lie detector tests?

options.A: They fully support the tests as a scientifically proven method to combat corruption.
options.B: They are confident that all new candidates will pass the tests.
options.C: They express skepticism, questioning the scientific validity of polygraphs.
options.D: They believe the tests are a necessary step towards developing the country.

questions.explanation: One of the managers of Gen Z Madagascar’s social media accounts expressed skepticism, stating, 'It’s not even scientifically proven to work,' and calling the idea 'just a joke and embarrassing'.

questions.stem: Despite being rich in natural resources, what is a major challenge Madagascar faces, as indicated by the provided data?

options.A: A high GDP per capita but low levels of natural resources.
options.B: Low rankings in corruption perceptions.
options.C: Being one of the world's poorest countries with a low GDP per capita.
options.D: Exploitation of resources by foreign powers.

questions.explanation: The article highlights that 'Madagascar is one of the world’s poorest countries, with a GDP per capita of just $545 (£408) in 2024'. While the exploitation of resources is mentioned as a problem, the direct economic indicator presented is its poverty status and low GDP per capita. The corruption perception index is also mentioned but the question asks about a 'major challenge' indicated by the data, and the poverty level is a more direct indicator of economic hardship.

papers.title: Some of the world’s poorest countries to lose UK aid due to 56% budget cut
papers.body_text: Some of the world’s poorest countries will lose out on UK aid that funds programmes such as schools and clinics, due to budget cuts set out by the foreign secretary. The UK’s bilateral aid to African countries will be reduced by almost £900m by 2028-29 – a 56% cut – as part of more than £6bn in cuts which are funding an increase in defence spending. Aid agencies said the cuts would be the steepest in the G7, leaving “the UK’s reputation in tatters, and a poorer, more unequal and unstable world for us all”. Labour MPs have privately expressed scepticism that the cuts have done much to achieve their desired intention – to bolster UK military spending in an uncertain world – because of the long delay in the defence investment plan and demands for billions more in spending from military chiefs in the wake of the Iran-US conflict. The 40% reduction in UK aid spending, which MPs voted to back last year, will mean all aid spending being cut to all G20 countries except Turkey, and the majority now focused on conflict zones, primarily Palestine, Sudan and Ukraine. Spending will be protected this year for Lebanon, a decision signed off by officials on Wednesday night, because of the intensity of the current offensive from Israel. The overhaul means 70% of all support will be allocated to the most fragile and conflict-affected states by 2029. Countries such as Afghanistan, Somalia and Yemen will be among those facing cuts, though Yvette Cooper, the foreign secretary, said they would still receive funding from multinational aid agencies. Countries such as Mozambique and Pakistan will have almost all their development aid cut, replaced by partnerships for investment. The crisis reserve for humanitarian emergencies has also been cut, though by less than expected, from £85m to £75m. “This for us is not an ideological step – it is a difficult choice in the face of international threats,” Cooper said. But Romilly Greenhill, CEO of Bond, the UK network for NGOs said: “Africa and the Middle East, both home to some of the world’s least-developed countries, will be forced to pay the highest price because of the reduced budget.” In its analysis of the impact assessment, Bond said the government’s own data showed the cuts would leave children, people with disabilities and older people more vulnerable across Ethiopia, Mozambique, Rwanda, Tanzania and Zambia and that fewer girls and children with disabilities will be able to go to school in South Sudan. Cuts to programmes in Somalia, one of the world’s most unstable countries, are likely to heavily affect the access to health services for women and children. The most significant impact will be felt across Africa, with bilateral overseas development aid due to fall from £818m in 2026 to £677m by 2029, which the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office said was part of a pivot to multilateral contributions through the World Bank and African Development Bank. The FCDO will also phase out all funding for bilateral programmes in G20 countries – apart from a small allocation to refugee-hosting in Turkey. No direct aid will go to countries such as Brazil, India, Indonesia and South Africa. The development minister, Jenny Chapman, said some of the poorest African nations that would feel the brunt of the cuts, such as Malawi, Mozambique and Sierra Leone, had expressed a preference for expertise partnerships with the UK, building stable financial systems and clean energy, rather than traditional aid programmes. “I think the concern that happened a year ago around the cuts was that people thought we were doing this because we lost faith in the agenda, we were turning our backs on the world … that this was a values shift. It’s absolutely not,” she said. “We’ve undertaken this task … in a very collaborative way with our global south partners. We’ve been very open about it. We’ve listened hard to what people have told us. We’ve been present. We’ve shown up just about everywhere we can, to have these conversations internationally.” But some Labour MPs were critical, with Fleur Anderson, the MP for Putney, saying: “The government has on one hand increased defence spending in response to a more dangerous world, but on the other cut the investment that helps build stability before crises emerge. “A serious approach must place development spending at the heart of global resilience and security. Without this, we are not preventing crises; we are simply waiting for them.” Admitting she was having to make hard choices on aid, Cooper said the UK still expected to be the fifth-biggest funder in the world, but in her statement she avoided spelling out the precise level of cuts, detail revealed only in the equality impact assessments. The FCDO has said the changes will prioritise geopolitical security and conflict – as well as funding the bigger multinational agencies, such as the vaccine programme Gavi. Funding is also being protected for the British Council and the BBC World Service. The UK has ringfenced £240m a year until 2029, alongside billions in loan guarantees for Ukraine, as well as protecting allocations for Palestine and Lebanon at current levels, with the latter explicitly funded to “reduce the drivers of irregular migration”. The cuts will also end aid to some major funders, including polio eradication and the Pandemic Fund. The cost of housing asylum seekers in UK hotels – running at roughly £2bn a year – is taken from the aid budget. It means that by 2027-28, aid spending on overseas programmes is expected to reach its lowest since records began in 1970, at just 0.24% of gross national income. Chapman said it was a wholesale overhaul of the way aid spending would now operate, after the decision to cut the aid budget despite a 0.7% target being legally enshrined. Cooper said it was the government’s intention to gradually return to the target when possible. Adrian Lovett, UK executive director of the ONE Campaign, said: “Today’s figures lay bare the true scale of these cuts and the damage they will do. Slashing bilateral aid to Africa, where need is greatest, will have a devastating impact. “These choices will leave millions without access to basic healthcare, education and urgent humanitarian support, and risk a resurgence of deadly diseases we’ve spent decades trying to fight.”

questions.stem: According to the text, what is the primary reason cited for the UK's reduction in foreign aid spending?

options.A: A shift in foreign policy priorities towards domestic issues.
options.B: An increase in defense spending to address international threats.
options.C: Economic recession and a need to cut all government expenditures.
options.D: A strategic decision to focus aid solely on G7 countries.

questions.explanation: The article explicitly states that the cuts are 'funding an increase in defence spending' and that the foreign secretary referred to it as a 'difficult choice in the face of international threats'.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a significant consequence of the UK's aid budget cuts, as highlighted by aid agencies and analysts?

options.A: An immediate improvement in the stability of recipient countries.
options.B: A boost in the UK's international reputation and influence.
options.C: Increased vulnerability for children, people with disabilities, and older people in affected nations.
options.D: A redirection of funds towards private sector investment partnerships in all G20 countries.

questions.explanation: The text mentions that 'the government’s own data showed the cuts would leave children, people with disabilities and older people more vulnerable across Ethiopia, Mozambique, Rwanda, Tanzania and Zambia' and that 'fewer girls and children with disabilities will be able to go to school in South Sudan'.

questions.stem: The article notes that while overall aid spending is reduced, certain regions and countries will receive protected or prioritized funding. Which of the following is an example of such a protected allocation?

options.A: All G20 countries except Turkey will continue to receive substantial bilateral aid.
options.B: Afghanistan, Somalia, and Yemen will see their funding levels maintained or increased.
options.C: Lebanon's aid spending will be protected due to the intensity of the current offensive from Israel.
options.D: Mozambique and Pakistan will receive increased development aid to foster new partnerships.

questions.explanation: The article states, 'Spending will be protected this year for Lebanon, a decision signed off by officials on Wednesday night, because of the intensity of the current offensive from Israel.'

questions.stem: According to the development minister, Jenny Chapman, what is the stated preference of some of the poorest African nations affected by the cuts?

options.A: A complete cessation of all UK aid and a move towards self-sufficiency.
options.B: Increased traditional aid programs focused on immediate humanitarian relief.
options.C: Partnerships focused on expertise, building stable financial systems, and clean energy.
options.D: A return to the 0.7% aid spending target as soon as possible.

questions.explanation: The minister is quoted as saying that some nations 'had expressed a preference for expertise partnerships with the UK, building stable financial systems and clean energy, rather than traditional aid programmes.'

questions.stem: The article suggests a significant shift in the UK's approach to foreign aid. What is a key element of this overhaul?

options.A: A complete withdrawal from multilateral contributions and a focus on bilateral aid.
options.B: Prioritizing humanitarian emergencies with an increased crisis reserve fund.
options.C: A pivot towards multilateral contributions through international financial institutions and a focus on conflict zones.
options.D: Ending all funding for the British Council and BBC World Service to reallocate resources.

questions.explanation: The text mentions that the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) said the reduction in bilateral overseas development aid was 'part of a pivot to multilateral contributions through the World Bank and African Development Bank' and that 'the changes will prioritise geopolitical security and conflict – as well as funding the bigger multinational agencies'.
